dn34:1.1:
1
Thus have I heard.
Once the Lord was staying at Campā beside the Gaggarā lotus-pond, with a large company of some five hundred monks. Then the Venerable Sāriputta addressed the monks: 'Friends, monks!' 'Friend!' replied the monks. And the Venerable Sāriputta said:
dn34:1.2:
2
'In growing groups from one to ten I'll teach
Dhamma for the gaining of Nibbāna,
That you may make an end of suffering,
And be free from all the ties that bind.
dn34:1.3:
3
'There is friends, (1) one thing that greatly helps
(bahukāro)
, (2) one thing to be developed
(bhāvetabbo)
, (3) one thing to be thoroughly known
(parin͂n͂eyyo)
, (4) one thing to be abandoned
(paha͂tabbo)
, (5) one thing that conduces to diminution
(ha͂na-bha͂giyo)
, (6) one thing that conduces to distinction
(visesa-bha͂giyo)
, (7) one thing hard to penetrate
(duppativijjho )
, (8) one thing to be made to arise
(uppa͂detabbo)
, (9) one thing to be thoroughly learnt
(abhin͂n͂eyyo)
, and (10) one thing to be realised
(sacchikātabbo)
.
dn34:2.1:
4
(1) 'Which one thing greatly helps? Tirelessness in wholesome states
(appamādo kusalesu dhammesu)
.
dn34:2.2:
5
(2) 'Which one thing is to be developed? Mindfulness with regard to the body, accompanied by pleasure
(kāya-gata sati sāta-sahagatā)
.
dn34:2.3:
6
(3) 'Which one thing is to be thoroughly known? Contact as a condition of the corruptions and of grasping
(phasso sāsavo upādāniyo)
.
dn34:2.4:
7
(4) 'Which one thing is to be abandoned? Ego-conceit
(asmimāna)
.
dn34:2.5:
8
(5) 'Which one thing conduces to diminution? Unwise attention
(ayoniso manasikāro)
.
dn34:2.6:
9
(6) 'Which one things conduces to distinction? Wise attention
(yoniso manasikāro)
.
dn34:2.7:
10
(7) 'Which one thing is hard to penetrate? Uninterrupted mental concentration
(ānantariko ceto-samādhi)
.
dn34:2.8:
11
(8) 'Which one thing is to be made to arise? Unshakeable knowledge
(akuppaṁ nāa͂ṇaṁ)
.
dn34:2.9:
12
(9) 'Which one thing is to be thoroughly learnt? All beings are maintained by nutriment.
dn34:2.10:
13
(10) 'Which one thing is to be realised? Unshakeable deliverance of mind
(akuppa͂ ceto-vimutti)
.
dn34:2.11:
14
'That makes ten things that are real and true, so and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathagata.'
dn34:2.12:
15
'Two things greatly help, (2) two things to be developed, (3) two things to be thoroughly known, (4) two things to be abandoned, (5) two things that conduce to diminution, (6) two things that conduce to distinction, (7) two things hard to penetrate, (8) two things to be made to arise, (9) two things to be thoroughly learn, and (10) two things to be realised.
dn34:2.13:
16
(1) 'Which two things greatly help? Mindfulness and clear awareness.
dn34:3.1:
17
(2) 'Which two things are to be developed? Calm and insight.
dn34:4.1:
18
(3) 'Which two things are to be thoroughly known? Mind and body.
dn34:4.2:
19
(4) 'Which two things are to be abandoned? Ignorance and craving for existence.
dn34:4.3:
20
(5) 'Which two things conduce to diminution? Roughness and friendship with evil.
dn34:4.4:
21
(6) 'Which two things conduce to distinction? Gentleness and friendship with good.
dn34:4.5:
22
(7) 'Which two things are hard to penetrate? That which is the root, the condition of the defilement of beings, and that which is the root, the condition of the purification of beings
(yo ca hetu yo ca paccayo sattā̵̵̵naṁ saṁkilesāya, …sattānaṁ visuddhiyā)
.
dn34:4.6:
23
(8) 'Which two things are to be made to arise? Knowledge of the destruction
and of
non-recurrence.
dn34:4.7:
24
(9) 'Which two things are to be thoroughly learnt? Two elements, the conditioned and the unconditioned
(saṅkhatā ca dhātu asaṅkhatā ca dhātu)
.
dn34:4.8:
25
(10) 'Which two things are to be realised? Knowledge and liberation.
dn34:4.9:
26
'That makes twenty things that are real and true, so and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathagata.'
dn34:4.10:
27
'Three things greatly help, three things are to be developed …
dn34:4.11:
28
(1)'Which three things greatly help? Association with good people, hearing the true Dhamma, practice of the Dhamma in its entirety.
dn34:4.12:
29
(2) 'Which three things are to be developed? Three kinds of concentration.
dn34:5.1:
30
(3) 'Which three things are to be thoroughly known? Three feelings.
dn34:5.2:
31
(4) 'Which three things are to be abandoned? Three kinds of craving.
dn34:5.3:
32
(5) 'Which three things conduce to diminution? Three unwholesome roots.
dn34:5.4:
33
(6) 'Which three things conduce to distinction? Three wholesome roots.
dn34:6.1:
34
(7) 'Which three things are hard to penetrate? Three elements making for deliverance
(nissāraṇīyā dhātuyo)
: deliverance from sensuality
(kāmā)
, that is, renunciation
(nekkhammaṁ)
, deliverance from material forms
(rāpā)
, that is, the immaterial
(āruppaṁ)
, whatever has become, is compounded, is conditionally arisen — the deliverance from that is cessation
(nirodho)
.
dn34:6.2:
35.
(8) 'Which three things are to be made to arise? Three knowledges
(ñāṇāni)
of past, future, present.
dn34:7.1:
36
(9) 'Which three things are to be thoroughly learnt? Three elements.
dn34:7.2:
37
(10) 'Which three things are to be realised? Three knowledges.
dn34:7.3:
38
'That makes thirty things that are real and true, so and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathagata.'
dn34:7.4:
39
'Four things greatly help, four things are to be developed…
dn34:7.5:
40
(1) 'Which four things greatly help? Four "wheels"
(cakkāni)
: a favourable place of residence
(paṭirūpa-desa-vāso)
, association with good people
(sappurisûpassayo)
, perfect development of one's personality
(atta-sammā-paṇidhi)
, past meritorious actions
(pubbe-kata-puññata)
.
dn34:8.1:
41
(2) 'Which four things are to be developed? Four foundations of mindfulness.
dn34:8.2:
42
(3) 'Which four things are to be thoroughly known? Four nutriments.
dn34:9.1:
43
(4) 'Which four things are to be abandoned? Four floods.
dn34:9.2:
44
(5)'Which four things conduce to diminution? Four yokes.
dn34:9.3:
45
(6) 'Which four things conduce to distinction? Four "unyokings".
dn34:10.1:
46
(7) 'Which four things are hard to penetrate? Four concentrations:
conducing to decline
,
conducing to stasis
,
conducive to distinction
,
conducive to penetration
.
dn34:10.2:
47
(8) 'Which four things are to be made to arise? Four knowledges.
dn34:11.1:
48
(9) 'Which four things are to be thoroughly learnt? Four Noble Truths.
dn34:12.1:
49
(10) 'Which four things are to be realised? Four fruits of the ascetic life.
dn34:12.2:
50
'That makes forty things that are real and true, so and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathāgata.
dn34:12.3:
51
'Five things greatly help, five things are to be developed…
dn34:13.1:
52
(1) 'Which five things greatly help? Five factors of endeavour.
dn34:14.1:
53
(2) 'Which five things are to be developed? Fivefold perfect concentration:
(a) suffusion with delight,
(pīti)
, (b) suffusion with happiness
(sukha)
, (c) suffusion with will
(ceto)
,
(d) suffusion with light
(āloka)
, (e) the "reviewing" sign
(paccavekkhaṇa-nimitta)
.
dn34:15.1:
54
(3) 'Which five things are to be thoroughly known? Five aggregates of grasping.
dn34:16.1:
55
(4) 'Which five things are to be abandoned? Five hindrances.
dn34:17.1:
56
(5) 'Which five things conduce to diminution? Five mental blockages.
dn34:18.1:
57
(6) 'Which five things conduce to distinction? Five faculties.
dn34:19.1:
58–62
(7) 'Which five things are hard to penetrate? Five elements making for deliverance.
dn34:20.1:
63
(8) 'Which five things are to be made to arise? The fivefold knowledge of right concentration
(pañcañāṇiko sammā samādhi)
: the knowledge that arises within one that:
dn34:21.1:
"This concentration is both present happiness and productive of future resultant happiness"
(āyatiñ ca sukha-vipāko)
,
dn34:21.2:
"This concentration is Ariyan and free from worldliness"
(nirdmiso)
,
dn34:21.3:
"This concentration is not practised by the unworthy"
(akāpurisasevito)
,
dn34:21.4:
"This concentration is calm and perfect, has attained tranquillisation, has attained unification, and is not instigated,
it cannot be denied
or prevented",
dn34:21.5: "I myself attain this concentration with mindfulness, and emerge from it with mindfulness."
dn34:22.1:
64–68
(9) 'Which five things are to be thoroughly learnt? Five bases of deliverance.
dn34:23.1:
69
(10) 'Which five things are to be realised? Five branches of Dhamma plus knowledge and vision of liberation
.
dn34:24.1:
70
'That makes fifty things that are real and true, and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathgāta.'
dn34:24.2:
71
'Six things greatly help, six things are to be developed…
dn34:25.1:
72–77
(1) 'Which six things greatly help? Six things to be remembered.
dn34:26.1:
78
(2) 'Which six things are to be developed? Six subjects of recollection.
dn34:26.2:
79
(3) 'Which six things are to be thoroughly known? Six internal sense-spheres.
dn34:27.1:
80
(4) 'Which six things are to be abandoned? Six groups of craving.
dn34:27.2:
81
(5) 'Which six things conduce to diminution? Six kinds of disrespect.
dn34:27.3:
82
(6) 'Which six things conduce to distinction? Six kinds of respect.
dn34:28.1:
83–88
(7) 'Which six things are hard to penetrate? Six elements making for deliverance.
dn34:29.1:
89
(8) 'Which six things are to be made to arise? Six stable states.
dn34:29.2:
90
(9) 'Which six things are to be thoroughly known? Six unsurpassed things.
dn34:30.1:
91
(10) 'Which six things are to be realised? Six super-knowledges
(abhiññā)
: Here, a monk applies and bends his mind to, and enjoys, different supernormal powers
(iddhī)
dn34:30.2: Being one, he becomes many and having been many, he becomes one; he appears and vanishes; he goes unimpeded through walls, ramparts, and mountains as if through space; he dives in and out of the earth as if it were water; he walks on water without sinking as if it were earth; sitting cross-legged he travels through space like a winged bird; with his hand he touches and strokes the sun and the moon, so mighty and powerful; he exercises mastery over the body as far as the Brahma-world.;
dn34:30.3:
92
With the divine ear-element, which is purified and surpasses the human, he hears both kinds of sound, the divine and the human, those which are distant and those which are near;
dn34:30.4:
93
He understands the minds of other beings and persons, having encompassed them with his own mind. He understands an unexalted mind as an exalted mind, a mind with lust as a mind with lust and an unliberated mind as an unliberated mind;
dn34:31.1:
94
He recollects his numerous past lives, that is, one birth, .. Thus he recollects his numerous past lives in their modes and their details;
dn34:31.2:
95
Thus with the divine eye, which is purified and surpasses the human, he sees beings passing away and reappearing—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, fortunate and unfortunate—and he understands how beings fare in accordance with their kamma;
dn34:31.3:
96
He abides, in this life, by his own super-knowledge and realisation, in the attainment of the corruptionless liberation of heart and liberation through wisdom.
dn34:31.4:
97
'That makes sixty things that are real and true, so and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathagata.'
dn34:31.5:
98
'Seven things help greatly, seven things are to be developed …
dn34:32.1:
99
(1) 'Which seven things greatly help? Seven treasures.
dn34:32.2:
100
(2) 'Which seven things are to be developed? Seven factors of enlightenment.
dn34:33.1:
101–107
(3) 'Which seven things are to be thoroughly known? Seven stations of consciousness.
dn34:34.1:
108
(4) 'Which seven things are to be abandoned? Seven latent proclivities.
dn34:34.2:
109
(5) 'Which seven things conduce to diminution? Seven wrong practices.
dn34:34.3:
110
(6) 'Which seven things conduce to distinction? Seven right practices.
dn34:34.4:
111
(7) 'Which seven things are hard to penetrate? Seven qualities of the true man.
dn34:34.5:
112
(8) 'Which seven things are to be made to arise? Seven perceptions.
dn34:35.1:
113
(9) 'Which seven things are to be thoroughly learnt? Seven grounds for commendation.
dn34:36.1:
114
(10) 'Which seven things are to be learnt? Seven powers of an Arahant
(khīnāsava-balāni)
. Here, for a monk who has destroyed the corruptions,
the impermanence of all compounded things is well seen, as it really is, by perfect insight. This is one way whereby he recognises that for him the corruptions are destroyed;
dn34:36.2:
115
… sense-desires are well seen as being like a pit of glowing embers…;
dn34:36.3:
116
… his heart
is bent on and inclined towards detachment
, slopes towards detachment and detachment is its object; rejoicing in renunciation
, his heart is totally unreceptive to all things pertaining to the corruptions…;
dn34:37.1:
117
… the four foundations of mindfulness have been well and truly developed … ;
dn34:38.1:
120
the Noble Eightfold Path has been well and truly developed … This is one of the powers whereby he recognises that for him the corruptions are destroyed.
dn34:39.1:
121
'That makes seventy things that are real and true, so and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathagata.'
dn34:39.3:
122
'Eight things greatly help, eight things are to be developed …
dn34:40.1:
123
(1) 'Which eight things greatly help? Eight causes, eight conditions conduce to wisdom in the fundamentals of the holy life, to gaining what has not been gained and to increasing, expanding and developing what has been gained. Here, one lives close to the Teacher or to a fellow-monk with the standing of a teacher, being thus strongly established in moral shame and moral dread, in love and veneration…
dn34:40.2:
124
He who is so placed from time to time goes to his teacher, asks and interrogates him: "How is that, Lord? What does this mean?" Thus his venerable teachers can reveal what is hidden and clarify obscurities, in this way helping him to solve his problems.
dn34:40.3:
125
Then, having heard Dhamma from them, he achieves withdrawal
,
of body and mind.
dn34:40.4:
126
Further, a monk is moral, he lives restrained according to the restraint of the discipline, persisting in right behaviour, seeing danger in the slightest fault, and keeping to the rules of training.
dn34:40.5:
127
Also, a monk, having learnt much, remembers and bears in mind what he has learnt, and those things that are beautiful in the beginning, in the middle and in the ending, which in spirit and letter proclaim the absolutely perfected and purified holy life, he remembers and reflects on, and penetrates them with vision.
dn34:40.6:
128
Again, a monk, having stirred up energy, continues to dispel unwholesome states, striving strongly and firmly, and not casting off the yoke of the wholesome.
dn34:40.7:
129
Again, a monk is mindful, with the highest mindfulness and discrimination, remembering and bearing in mind what has been done or said in the past.
dn34:40.8:
130
Also, a monk continually contemplates the rise and fall of the five aggregates of grasping, thinking: "Such is material form, its arising and passing; such are feelings, such are perceptions, such are the mental formations, such is consciousness, its arising and passing."
dn34:41.1:
131
(2) 'Which eight things are to be developed? The Noble Eightfold Path: Right View…Right Concentration.
dn34:41.2:
132
(3) 'Which eight things are to be thoroughly known? Eight worldly conditions.
dn34:41.3:
133
(4) 'Which eight things are to be abandoned? Eight wrong factors.
dn34:42.1:
134–141
(5) 'Which eight things conduce to diminution? Eight occasions of indolence.
dn34:43.1:
142–149
(6) 'Which eight things conduce to distinction? Eight occasions for making an effort.
dn34:44.1:
150–157
(7) 'Which eight things are hard to penetrate? Eight unfortunate, inopportune times for leading the holy life.
dn34:45.1:
158
(8) 'Which eight things are to be made to arise? Eight thoughts of a Great Man
:
"This Dhamma is for one of few wants, not one of many wants; for the contented, not for the discontented; for the withdrawn, not for those delighting in company; for the energetic, not for the lazy; for one of established mindfulness, not for one of lax mindfulness; for one of concentrated mind, not for one who is not concentrated; for one who has wisdom, not for one lacking wisdom; for one who delights in non-proliferation
(nippapañcārāmassa)
, not for one who delights in proliferation."
dn34:46.1:
159–166
(9) 'Which eight things are to be thoroughly learnt? Eight states of mastery.
dn34:47.1:
167–174
(10) 'Which eight things are to be realised? Eight liberations.
dn34:48.1:
175
'That makes eighty things that are real and true, so and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathagata.'
dn34:48.2:
176
'Nine things greatly help, nine things are to be developed …
dn34:48.3:
177
(1) 'Which nine things greatly help? Nine conditions rooted in wise consideration
: When a monk practises wise consideration, joy
arises in him, and from his being joyful, delight
arises, and from his feeling delight, his senses
are calmed; as a result of this calming he feels happiness
, and from his feeling happy, his mind becomes concentrated; with his mind thus concentrated, he knows and sees things as they really are; with his thus knowing and seeing things as they really are, he becomes disenchanted
; with disenchantment he becomes dispassionate
, and by dispassion he is liberated.
dn34:48.4:
178
(2) 'Which nine things are to be developed? Nine factors of the effort for perfect purity
: the factor of effort for purity of morality, … for purity of mind, … for purity of view, … of purification by overcoming doubt
, …of purification by knowledge and vision of path and not-path $
, …of purification by knowledge and vision of progress
, … of purification by knowledge and vision
, … of purity of wisdom
, … of purity of deliverance
.
dn34:49.1:
179–187
(3) 'Which nine things are to be thoroughly known? Nine abodes of beings.
dn34:50.1:
188
(4) 'Which nine things are to be abandoned? Nine things rooted in craving: in dependence upon feeling there is craving; in dependence upon craving there is pursuit; in dependence upon pursuit there is gain; in dependence upon gain there is decision-making; in dependence upon decision-making there is desire and lust; in dependence upon desire and lust there is attachment; in dependence upon attachment there is possessiveness; in dependence upon possessiveness there is stinginess; in dependence upon stinginess there is safeguarding; and because of safeguarding, various evil unwholesome phenomena originate—the taking up of clubs and weapons, conflicts, quarrels, and disputes, insulting speech, slander, and falsehoods.
dn34:51.1:
189
(5) 'Which nine things conduce to diminution? Nine causes of malice.
dn34:52.1:
190
(6) 'Which nine things conduce to distinction? Nine ways of overcoming malice.
dn34:53.1:
191
(7) 'Which nine things are hard to penetrate? Nine differences
: Owing to difference of element
there is difference of contact
; owing to difference of contact there is difference of feeling; owing to difference of feeling there is difference of perception; owing to difference of perception there is difference of thought
; owing to difference of thought there is difference of intention
; owing to difference of intention there is difference of obsession
; owing to difference of obsession there is difference of quest
; owing to difference of quest there is difference of what is gained
.
dn34:53.2:
192
(8) 'Which nine things are to be made to arise? Nine perceptions
:of the foul
, of death,
of the loathsomeness of food
, of distaste for the whole world
, of impermanence, of the suffering in impermanence, of impersonality in suffering, of relinquishment
, of dispassion
.
dn34:54.1:
193
(9) 'Which nine things are to be thoroughly learnt? Nine successive abidings.
dn34:55.1:
194
(10) 'Which nine things are to be realised? Nine successive cessations.
dn34:56.1:
195
'That makes ninety things that are real and true so and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathāgata.'
dn34:56.2:
196
'Ten things (1) greatly help, (2) are to be developed, (3) are to be thoroughly known, (4) are to be abandoned, (5) conduce to diminution, (6) conduce to distinction, (7) are hard to penetrate, (8) are to be made to arise, (9) are to be thoroughly learnt, (10) are to be realised.
dn34:57.1:
197–206
(1) 'Which ten things greatly help? Ten things that give protection.
dn34:58.1:
207
(2) 'Which ten things are to be developed? Ten objects for the attainment of absorption.
dn34:59.1:
208
(3) 'Which ten things are to be thoroughly known? Ten sense-spheres
(ādyatanāni)
: eye and sight-object, ear and sound, nose and smell, tongue and taste, body and tactile object.
dn34:59.2:
209
(4) 'Which ten things are to be abandoned? Ten wrong courses plus wrong knowledge
and wrong liberation
.
dn34:60.1:
210
(5) 'Which ten things conduce to diminution? Ten unwholesome courses of action.
dn34:60.2:
211
(6) 'Which ten things conduce to distinction? Ten wholesome courses of action.
dn34:61.1:
212–222
(7) 'Which ten things are hard to penetrate? Ten Ariyan dispositions
dn34:62.1:
223
(8) 'Which ten things are to be made to arise? Ten perceptions
:of the foul
, of death, of the loathsomeness of food
, of distaste for the whole world
, of impermanence, of the suffering in impermanence, of impersonality in suffering, of relinquishment
, of dispassion
and the perception of cessation
.
dn34:62.2:
224
(9) 'Which ten things are to be thoroughly learnt? Ten causes of wearing-away
: By right view wrong view is worn away, and whatever evil and unwholesome states arise on the basis of wrong view are worn away too. And by right view many wholesome states are developed and perfected. By right thought wrong thought is worn away… By right speech wrong speech is worn away… By right action wrong action is worn away… By right livelihood wrong livelihood is worn away … By right effort wrong effort is worn away … By right mindfulness wrong mindfulness is worn away… By right concentration wrong concentration is worn away … By right knowledge
wrong knowledge is worn away … By right liberation wrong liberation is worn away, and whatever evil and unwholesome states arise on the basis of wrong liberation are worn away too. And by right liberation many wholesome states are developed and perfected.
dn34:63.1:
225
(10) 'Which ten things are to be realised? Ten qualities of the non-learner.
dn34:64.1:
226
'That makes a hundred things that are real and true, so and not otherwise, unerringly and perfectly realised by the Tathāgata.'
dn34:64.2: So said the Venerable Sāriputta. And the monks were delighted and rejoiced at his words.
